spec_match.rationale: Reproduces the `newwindow` event interception pattern with a real glob matcher; the code block shows the exact `e.preventDefault()` shape.

interactive.rationale: Rules textarea, URL input, dry-run button, allow/block history list with reasons — past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises whitelist + dry-run; the page does that.

edge_cases.rationale: Globs work but no demonstrations of: same-origin redirects, fragment-only navigations, target=_blank, javascript: schemes. The kiosk threat model is full of those.

references.rationale: Includes the correct chromestatus.com/feature/5199572022853632 link and the Controlled Frame spec.

design_system.rationale: Variables only, allow/block use accent-emerald/rose, no static-card.

openQuestions.title: Add hostile pattern test cases
openQuestions.detail: Pre-seed the input list with attacker payloads: `javascript:alert(1)`, `data:text/html,<script>`, scheme-confused `httpss://example.com`, mixed-case domains, IDN homoglyphs. Visitors should see what their whitelist permits / blocks without authoring them by hand.

openQuestions.title: Distinguish newwindow vs in-frame navigation
openQuestions.detail: The interceptor pattern differs between `newwindow` (popup, target=_blank) and `loadcommit` (in-place nav). Right now the tester treats them as one. Add a radio (or two columns) for which event the URL would fire.

summary: Useful glob playground that captures the host-side interceptor pattern. To level up, model the attack surface explicitly — hostile URLs and the newwindow-vs-loadcommit split are where real kiosk bugs hide.
